title: The AI capital cycle
question: Is the 2024-26 AI buildout a capital cycle in its money-burning phase, held together by circular vendor financing - and if so, where on the historical clock are we, and what breaks the circle?
finding: Mid-to-late frenzy, and the tell is positional. 'They lose money' is true only in a precise form: serving a token is gross-margin-positive (OpenAI ~33%, Anthropic ~40%), but the heavy-tail flat-rate subscriber is negative by construction and the all-in burn is a choice until commitments make it contractual (OpenAI carries ~$1.15T of compute commitments against ~$13B of 2025 revenue). The financing has crossed from cash to equity to debt and SPVs in 2025-26 - the same late-cycle progression that preceded the 1847 and 2000 tops - while a Greenwood-Shleifer-You >100% two-year-net-of-market screen, computed from adjusted closes, flags 8 of 17 AI names (MU +704, PLTR +529, GEV +425, INTC +221, VRT +207, AMD +154, AVGO +144, TSM +122 points of excess) but NOT Nvidia (+34), with Microsoft (-35) and Meta (-8) already negative: the mania has migrated downstream to memory, power and the application layer while the bellwether shows no blow-off. The break trigger is a financing refusal, not a demand collapse; fragility concentrates in the leveraged periphery (CoreWeave-tier neoclouds, then Oracle as the credit channel, then OpenAI's funding gap) while TSMC, the FCF-rich hyperscalers and the monopoly chokepoints survive. Every historical analog - railways 1840s, telecom 1999, radio 1929 - separates 'the technology is real' from 'the capital is safe'; both were true at once each time. Extension (Jun 2026): SemiAnalysis measured the subscription subsidy directly by maxing every plan - a $200 seat draws $8,000 (Anthropic) to $14,000 (OpenAI) of API-equivalent compute, break-even utilization 5.7-20% - and the exposure ladder when the subsidy ends ranks subscription-priced agentic tooling and the heavy-tail prosumer first, the relay economy second, OpenAI (consumer-heavy mix, deepest subsidy at every tier) third, ahead of wrapper startups, neoclouds and the GSY-flagged downstream names; Anthropic and the hyperscalers are least exposed. How I'd know I'm wrong: if Nvidia's two-year excess clears the GSY flag (blow-off in the core), if hyperscaler capex guidance moderates before any credit event (demand-led not credit-led), or if neocloud GPUs re-let at ~95% of price through 2026 (no collateral problem).
